questions.question: Why can a modest-QPS service still have serious bandwidth and cost pressure?
options: Because large payload sizes can make byte throughput and egress cost high even when request count is not huge.
Because bandwidth matters only for storage systems.
Because low QPS always implies low egress.
Because cache hit rate does not affect throughput.
questions.explanation: Requests are only half the story; bytes per request often dominate.

questions.question: What clarification is most important before estimating origin egress?
options: Which whiteboard marker color the interviewer prefers.
How much traffic is absorbed by edge or CDN caches before reaching origin.
Whether the service uses SQL or NoSQL.
How many engineers work on the team.
questions.explanation: Cache hits directly reduce origin bandwidth and cost.

questions.question: Which estimate most directly determines peak total throughput?
options: Peak QPS multiplied by average payload size
Daily active users multiplied by retention days
Replication factor multiplied by write latency
CPU cores multiplied by cache hit rate
questions.explanation: Bandwidth is fundamentally request rate times bytes per response.

questions.question: If the edge cache hit rate increases, what usually happens to origin egress?
options: It usually decreases materially.
It always increases because more content is served.
It stays identical because hits and misses cost the same.
It affects only latency, not bandwidth.
questions.explanation: More hits at the edge mean fewer bytes fetched from origin.

questions.question: What is the hidden risk of relying on average payload size alone?
options: A small set of large objects may dominate total bytes and overload links or budgets.
Average payload size removes the need for cache modeling.
It guarantees underestimation of write QPS only.
It affects storage but not network planning.
questions.explanation: Byte-heavy tails can matter much more than average request count suggests.

questions.question: Which observability metric best checks whether cache is protecting origin bandwidth as planned?
options: Origin miss bytes and edge hit ratio by content class
Number of API endpoints
Number of engineers on call
Average sprint velocity
questions.explanation: You want direct evidence of how many bytes still reach origin.

questions.question: What trade-off does aggressive compression introduce?
options: Lower transfer size, but more CPU work and sometimes added latency.
Higher origin cost, but lower cache hit ratio.
Better retention efficiency, but lower write durability.
Lower queue depth, but worse consistency.
questions.explanation: Compression shifts cost from network to compute.

questions.question: When is separate regional egress modeling especially important?
options: When pricing or traffic distribution varies significantly across regions.
When all traffic is internal and free by definition.
When payload size is less than one kilobyte.
When the service has no caches at all.
questions.explanation: Regional pricing and traffic skew can make one global average misleading.
